coredata.dc:title: Fluorometric detection of quinolones via AIE and FRET with terbium-doped carbon dots and copper nanoclusters

coredata.dc:creator: Ali, Ramadan
Alshaman, Reem
Albalawi, Abdullah S.
Alsharif, Osama M.
Hakami, Osama A.
Alharthi, Hamzah Hussain
Jumah, Faisal F.
Al-Qarni, Abdulrhman M.
Albalawi, Naif S.
Alashjaee, Meshari Saud
Aljohani, Faisal S.
Alghuzawi, Abdulaziz M.
Al-Atwi, Muath H.
Alharbi, Abdullah H.
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
coredata.dc:description: Terbium and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Tb@N-CDs), combined with α-lipoic acid-functionalized copper nanoclusters (LA@CuNCs), were proposed for the ratiometric detection of quinolone (QA) antibiotics. In this system, Tb@N-CDs facilitate the aggregation of LA@CuNCs, enhancing its fluorescence emission at 670 nm via aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE). Meanwhile, the fluorescence emission of Tb@N-CDs at 460 nm diminishes due to Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). Upon the introduction of QA, the binding between Tb3+ ions and N-CDs weakens, disrupting both AIEE and FRET processes. This disruption results in a reduction in fluorescence emission at 670 nm and a concurrent increase at 460 nm. The fluorescence response ratio (F460/F670) increases with higher concentrations of ciprofloxacin (CFX), demonstrating a linear range from 0.008 to 120 μmol L−1 and LOD of 1.6 nmol L−1. The method successfully detected CFX in milk and urine samples, achieving recoveries between 97.7 % and 103.8 %, RSD of less than 3.79 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric detection
Blue and red emissions
Fluorescence detection
Milk analysis
Urine analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of synthesis and reaction conditions
Quality control of fluorometric and HPLC/UV methods

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Absorption spectra of LA@CuNCs, N-CDs, and Tb@N-CDs; (B) Fluorescence emission of Tb@N-CDs; (C) Emission-independency on the excitation wavelengths (280–360 nm).
(A) Absorption spectra of LA@CuNCs; (B) Excitation/fluorescence spectra of LA@CuNCs; (C) Emission-independency on the excitation wavelengths (280–360 nm).
Fluorescence spectra of LA@CuNCs, N-CDs, and Tb@N-CDs.
(A) Responses of the fluorescent system LA@CuNCs/Tb@N-CDs towards different amounts of CFX (0.05–120 μmolL−1); (B) Calibration plot.

The selectivity of LA@CuNCs/Tb@N-CDs fluorescent system towards detection of 30 μmolL−1 CFX and in the presence of 200 μmolL−1 of antibiotics, 250 μmolL−1 of common pesticides, and 400 μmolL−1 metal ions.
Recoveries of CFX in milk and urine samples by ratiometric fluorescent probe and HPLC/UV at three spiked concentrations (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Fluorometric detection of quinolones via AIE and FRET with terbium-doped carbon dots and copper nanoclusters

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut 71526, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Terbium and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Tb@N-CDs), combined with α-lipoic acid-functionalized copper nanoclusters (LA@CuNCs), were proposed for the ratiometric detection of quinolone (QA) antibiotics. In this system, Tb@N-CDs facilitate the aggregation of LA@CuNCs, enhancing its fluorescence emission at 670 nm via aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE). Meanwhile, the fluorescence emission of Tb@N-CDs at 460 nm diminishes due to Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). Upon the introduction of QA, the binding between Tb3+ ions and N-CDs weakens, disrupting both AIEE and FRET processes. This disruption results in a reduction in fluorescence emission at 670 nm and a concurrent increase at 460 nm. The fluorescence response ratio (F460/F670) increases with higher concentrations of ciprofloxacin (CFX), demonstrating a linear range from 0.008 to 120 μmol L−1 and LOD of 1.6 nmol L−1. The method successfully detected CFX in milk and urine samples, achieving recoveries between 97.7 % and 103.8 %, RSD of less than 3.79 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics play a crucial role in promoting animal growth and preventing diseases (Murugan & Natarajan, 2024). Among them, quinolones, such as ciprofloxacin (CFX), are widely utilized for treating gastrointestinal, soft tissue, and urinary tract infections (Mahmoud, Abu-Alrub, Al-Qarni, El-Wekil, & Shahin, 2024). Besides CFX, other quinolones like enrofloxacin, moxifloxacin, ofloxacin, norfloxacin, and gatifloxacin have also been identified in milk and urine samples (Liu et al., 2024). CFX was chosen as the target analyte for this study due to its extensive use in veterinary medicine and its frequent presence in food products. Research indicates that CFX is one of the most commonly detected quinolone residues in milk, underscoring its importance as a target for monitoring antibiotic contamination (Wang et al., 2025). Existing regulations impose strict limits on antibiotic residues in food products to safeguard consumer health. For instance, the European Union has established a maximum residue limit of 100 μg/kg for CFX in milk (Regulation EC No 37/2010). Similar regulatory frameworks are enforced by international bodies, including the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and Codex Alimentarius. The detection sensitivity of our method for CFX in milk and urine falls well below these regulatory thresholds, thereby supporting compliance with global food safety standards. Misuse of antibiotics, however, can lead to residual contamination in environmental waters and animal-based foods such as eggs, milk, and meat (Liu et al., 2024; Sharma et al., 2024), posing significant health and environmental risks. Detecting antibiotics in food and environmental samples is essential for ensuring public health and safety. Conventional detection methods, such as capillary electrophoresis (CE), electrochemical techniques, and High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC), often demand complex instrumentation, specialized expertise, and extensive sample preparation (Abdel-Gawad & Altharawi, 2023; Kheirandish et al., 2023; Yan, Zhou, Wang, & Huang, 2023). Moreover, while CE and HPLC are technically complex, electrochemical methods are challenged by selectivity issues and electrode fouling, which can compromise accuracy and reliability (Mahnashi et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2024; Alanazi et al., 2024).

Carbon dots (CDs) are quasi-spherical, zero-dimensional nanostructures composed of crystalline sp2‑carbon, incorporating hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino functional groups (Mahmoud et al., 2023). CDs find extensive applications in fields such as energy production, environmental remediation, photonic materials, imaging, and sensing (AlQarni, Mahmoud, Ali, & El-Wekil, 2023; Dehghani, Akhond, Jangi, & Absalan, 2024). They offer several advantages, including water solubility, photostability, physical stability, ease of fabrication, strong fluorescence, and accessible surface functionalization (Magdy, Said, El-Domany, & Belal, 2024; Mahnashi, Mahmoud, El-Wekil, & Shahin, 2023). Doping CDs with heteroatoms, such as nitrogen, further enhances their fluorescence quantum yield and coordination capacity (Gallareta-Olivares et al., 2023).

Metal nanoclusters are recognized for their unique electronic and optical properties, characterized by remarkable brightness attributed to discrete electronic structures and the quantum confinement effect (Tao, Wu, Zhang, & Wang, 2022; Wang, Tao, Wu, Zhang, & Liu, 2022). Copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) are particularly attractive as they are cost-effective, abundant, and environmentally friendly compared to gold and silver nanoclusters. However, CuNCs face stability challenges due to susceptibility to oxidation. Stabilizers, such as DNA, proteins, and thiolated ligands, can enhance their stability and extend their functional applications (Huang et al., 2017). These ligands also facilitate metal ion detection through the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effect (Alqahtani, Mahmoud, Ibrahim, & El-Wekil, 2024; Yuan et al., 2024).

This approach introduces a novel probe designed for the sensitive ratiometric fluorometric detection of QA. The probe integrates positively charged terbium and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Tb@N-CDs) with negatively charged α-lipoic acid-modified copper nanoclusters (LA@CuNCs). The interaction between Tb@N-CDs and LA@CuNCs is governed by two primary mechanisms: aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). In the presence of QA, such as ciprofloxacin (CFX), the Tb3+ ions on the N-CDs are chelated, which disrupts both AIEE and FRET processes. This disruption results in a decrease in LA@CuNC fluorescence and a corresponding restoration of Tb@N-CD fluorescence, yielding a ratiometric probe for effective QA detection.

Ciprofloxacin (CFX, 98.7 %), phenylenediamine (97.6 %), terbium chloride hexahydrate (TbCl3•6H2O), cyhalothrin (99.4 %), flonicamid (98.3 %), dinotefuran (97.2 %), chlormequat (97.4 %), propargite (99.2 %), bifenthrin (97.2 %), cypermethrin (96.4 %), novaluron (98.5 %), boscalid (98.4 %), tetracycline hydrochloride (99.7 %), ampicillin (99.8 %), sulfamethoxazole (98.8 %), oxytetracycline (99.8 %), sulfamethazine (98.7 %), α-lipoic acid (97.8 %), and Tris HCl buffer were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. KCl, NaCl, MgCl2, CoCl2, ZnCl2, FeCl2, HgNO3, CuCl2, PbCl2, CdCl2, BaCl2, AgCl, FeCl3, HCl, and NaOH were purchased from El-Nasser Chemicals for intermediate, Cairo, Egypt. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ•cm was used throughout the entire study. Milk samples were collected from local markets at Assiut City, Egypt.

Spectrofluorometric and spectrophotometric measurements were conducted using the Shimadzu RF-5301 PC and Shimadzu 1601 PC instruments (Tokyo, Japan), respectively. Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of LA@CuNCs and Tb@N-CDs were obtained using the NicoletTM iSTM10 (USA) and EscalAB 250Xi (USA) systems, respectively. Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were performed with the ZEN 3600 Nano ZS model (Malvern, UK). Surface morphology was analyzed through transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using a JEOL microscope (USA), while diffraction angles of LA@CuNCs and Tb@N-CDs were measured using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD) from Philips-FEI (Netherlands).

Initially, 20.0 mmol L−1 of α-lipoic acid (LA), solubilized in a mixture of ultrapure water and NaOH (3:9, v/v), was combined with an equal volume of 5.0 mM CuCl₂ and stirred for 10 min. The mixture was then heated at 170 °C for one hour. Finally, the dialyzed solution (molecular weight cut-off = 3000 Da) was lyophilized and stored at 4 °C.

Phenylenediamine (20.0 mg) and TbCl₃•6H₂O (60 mg) were dispersed in 50 mL of ultrapure water and sonicated for 15 min to achieve complete solubility. The mixture was subsequently heated at 220 °C for three hours. After cooling, it was centrifuged at 227.9 g for 20 min, followed by dialysis (molecular weight cut-off = 3000 Da). Finally, the dialyzed solution was lyophilized and stored at 4 °C.

A volume of 330 μL of 0.5 mg mL−1 Tb@N-CDs and 330 μL of 2.5 mg mL−1 LA@CuNCs were combined with 700 μL of Tris-HCl buffer (pH 6.8). The resulting solution was incubated at room temperature (RT) for one minute. Next, 100 μL of quinolone antibiotic solutions at varying concentrations (with ciprofloxacin as a representative example) was added. The mixture was then incubated at RT for three minutes before recording the fluorescence spectra at 330 nm.

Milk samples were collected in February 2024 from three different supermarkets in Assiut City, Egypt, with a total of three samples randomly selected to ensure source diversity. Each sample was placed in sterile containers and transported to the laboratory under refrigerated conditions (4 °C), then analyzed within 24 h to preserve analyte integrity.

Urine samples were obtained in February 2024 from three healthy volunteers from the research team in Assiut City, Egypt. Each sample was collected in sterile containers, centrifuged immediately after collection to remove particulate matter, and stored at −20 °C until analysis.

Samples were prepared following previously established methods (Ali, Hassan, Hassan, & El-Wekil, 2021; Mahmoud, Mahnashi, & El-Wekil, 2024; Alyami, Mahmoud, Alkahtani and El-Wekil, 2021). Five milliliters of milk sample were combined with varying concentrations of CFX and 2.5 mL of acetonitrile with 2 g of ZnCl₂ to precipitate proteins. The mixture was then centrifuged at 911.7 g for 20 min. Subsequently, 100 μL of the supernatant was combined with the fluorescent probe as detailed in Section 2.5.

For human urine samples (Mahnashi, Mahmoud, Alkahtani and El-Wekil, 2021), the collected urine was centrifuged at 227.9 g for 15 min. Then, 100 μL of the supernatant, with and without added concentrations of CFX, was mixed with the fluorescent probe as described in Section 2.5.

Quantification of CFX in milk and urine samples was conducted using an HPLC system with UV detection at 278 nm. Separation was achieved on a C18 column (250 × 4.6 mm, Kromasil) with a mobile phase of phosphate buffer (pH 2.7) and acetonitrile in a 77:23 volumetric ratio. The flow rate was maintained at 1.0 mL/min, with the column held at room temperature. A 10 μL volume of each sample was injected for analysis, and the retention time for CFX was approximately 4.83 min.

Milk samples were prepared by adding 5 mL of milk (spiked with varying amounts of CFX) to 2.5 mL of acetonitrile and 2 g of ZnCl₂ to precipitate proteins and fat. The mixture was vortexed for 2 min, then centrifuged at 911.7 g for 20 min. The clear supernatant was collected, filtered through a 0.22 μm syringe filter, and subsequently injected into the HPLC system for analysis.

Urine samples were prepared by centrifuging 5 mL of urine (spiked with varying amounts of CFX) at 227.9 g for 15 min to remove particulate matter. The resulting supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm syringe filter and directly injected into the HPLC system.

Quality control measures were implemented to ensure the accuracy, precision, and reproducibility of the fluorometric and HPLC/UV methods for CFX detection in milk and urine samples. Quality control (QC) involved spiking milk and urine samples with known CFX concentrations at low, medium, and high levels to evaluate recovery rates and confirm the method's accuracy. Each spiked sample was analyzed in triplicate to calculate mean recovery percentages and assess repeatability. Blank and.

QC samples were run alongside test samples to verify the absence of contamination or carryover. Calibration standards were analyzed at the beginning, middle, and end of each batch to ensure linearity across the concentration range and monitor for any drift in instrument response. Finally, daily instrument calibration and performance checks were conducted to ensure consistent and accurate fluorescence measurements.

All data were collected in triplicate and are presented as mean ± standard deviation. Data visualization was performed using Microsoft Excel 2010. Differences in results between the fluorometric and HPLC methods were assessed using a t-test at a 95 % confidence level in Microsoft Excel 2010. Data homogeneity was evaluated through Levene's test to confirm that variances between groups were not significantly different (p > 0.05), thereby validating the assumptions for the t-test. Fluorescence emissions of LA@CuNCs and Tb@N-CDs were measured at 670 nm and 460 nm, respectively. A calibration curve was generated by plotting the fluorescence ratio (F670/F460) against the concentration of CFX.

The morphology and surface structure of LA@CuNCs and Tb@N-CDs were investigated using a range of techniques, including FT-IR, XRD, TEM, XPS, EDX, UV/Vis spectrophotometry, and fluorescence spectroscopy. In Fig. S1A, the FT-IR spectra of LA and LA@CuNCs are presented. The FT-IR spectrum of LA shows absorption bands at 3340, 2800–2880, 1695, 1530, and 1310 cm−1, corresponding to υ (OH), υ (CH₂), υ (C=O), δ (OH), and υ (SS) groups, respectively (Alhazzani et al., 2024). These bands shift to lower frequencies upon binding to CuNCs, indicating complex formation. Fig. S1B illustrates the FT-IR spectra of N-CDs and Tb@N-CDs, where N-CDs exhibit absorption bands at 3420, 3280, 2885, 1675, 1570, and 580 cm−1, attributed to υ (OH), υ (NH), υ (CH₂), υ (C=O), and δ (OH/NH), respectively. Upon binding with Tb3+, these bands either disappear or decrease, with a new absorption band appearing at 580 cm−1 (δ (O-Tb-N)), confirming the formation of Tb@N-CDs (Mahmoud, Mahnashi, & El-Wekil, 2024).

In Fig. S2, the XRD patterns of LA@CuNCs and Tb@N-CDs are presented. The diffraction peaks of LA@CuNCs (Fig. S2A) appear at 26.8°, 37.8°, 52.3°, and 68.2°, corresponding to the (002), (111), (200), and (220) planes, respectively (Gao et al., 2024). In Fig. S2B, Tb@N-CDs show a characteristic diffraction peak at 22.3°, indicative of the graphitic structure of N-CDs (Alkahtani, Mahmoud, Alqahtani, Ali, & El-Wekil, 2023; Mahmoud, Abu-Alrub, Alqarni, El-Wekil, & Ali, 2023). TEM images in Fig.S3 reveal spherical nanoparticles for LA@CuNCs and Tb@N-CDs, with average diameters of 2.1 ± 0.08 nm and 4.0 ± 0.13 nm (n = 100), respectively.

XPS analysis in Fig. S4 provides further insights. Fig. S4A shows the XPS spectrum of LA@CuNCs, with distinct peaks at 163.5, 285.7, 398.2, 533.4, and 953.4 eV, corresponding to S 2p, C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and Cu 2p, respectively (Alanazi, Alhazzani, Mahmoud, Ali, & El-Wekil, 2024). Fig. S4B presents the deconvoluted S 2p spectra, with peaks at 162.3 eV and 163.7 eV for S 2p₃/₂ and S 2p₁/₂, respectively. Fig. S4C shows the deconvoluted Cu 2p spectra, with peaks at 932.8 and 954.6 eV, indicating Cu in valence states 0 and + 1 (Li et al., 2024). The XPS spectrum of Tb@N-CDs in Fig. S5A exhibits peaks at 284.7, 397.8, 531.3, and 1238.9 eV, attributed to C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and Tb 3d, respectively (Tian & Fan, 2022). Fig. S5B shows the deconvoluted Tb 3d spectra, with peaks at 1243.2 and 1278.5 eV, corresponding to Tb 3d₅/₂ and Tb 3d₃/₂, indicating the presence of Tb3+ and Tb4+ valence states.

The EDX spectra in Fig. S6 confirm the elemental composition of the nanoparticles. Fig. S6A displays the EDX spectrum of LA@CuNCs, showing peaks for carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, sulfur, and copper, with elemental percentages of 54.1 %, 12.8 %, 6.7 %, and 26.4 %, respectively. Fig.S6B shows the EDX spectrum of Tb@N-CDs, with peaks for carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and terbium, with respective percentages of 42.9 %, 32.2 %, 18.3 %, and 6.6 %. Stability patterns of LA@CuNCs and Tb@N-CDs under various conditions, including temperature, irradiation time, ionic strength, and pH, are demonstrated in Figs.S7 and S8. Photoluminescence quantum yields for LA@CuNCs and Tb@N-CDs were calculated as 13.62 % and 26.78 %, respectively (see supporting information).

Fig. 1A shows the UV/Vis absorption spectra of LA@CuNCs, N-CDs, and Tb@N-CDs. The LA@CuNCs probe exhibits weak absorption at 243 nm, likely due to the LA protective agent. N-CDs show absorption bands at 224, 267, and 322 nm, attributed to π-π* transitions, excited-state energy trapping, and n-π* transitions, respectively (Zhu, Zhang, Hu, & Wu, 2024). Binding of Tb3+ ions to N-CDs results in the disappearance of the 224 nm band and hypsochromic shifts in other bands, suggesting coordination with Tb3+ ions. Fig. 1B displays the excitation and fluorescence spectra of Tb@N-CDs at 320 and 460 nm, respectively. Fig. 1C confirms the excitation-independent behavior of Tb@N-CDs, showing consistent emission wavelengths across varying excitation wavelengths.

Fig. 2A illustrates the absorption spectrum of LA@CuNCs, showing a peak between 230 and 270 nm, attributed to the CuS bond (Zhang et al., 2023). Absence of absorption in the 400–750 nm range rules out the presence of large nanoparticles. Fig. 2B presents the excitation and emission spectra of LA@CuNCs at 320 nm and 670 nm, respectively. Fig. 2C demonstrates the excitation-independent behavior of LA@CuNCs, with stable emission wavelengths regardless of excitation variations.

Fig. 3 shows the fluorescence emission spectra of LA@CuNCs, N-CDs, and Tb@N-CDs. LA@CuNCs display no fluorescence in the 400–640 nm range, while N-CDs exhibit a fluorescence peak at 460 nm, which becomes enhanced and blue-shifted upon doping with Tb3+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of synthesis and reaction conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of various conditions on CFX detection was systematically examined, focusing on both synthesis and reaction parameters. Fabrication conditions for LA@CuNCs and Tb@N-CDs were optimized by adjusting the ratios of protecting agents to metal ions, heating temperatures, and heating durations. Fig.S9 illustrates the optimization process for LA@CuNCs, identifying the ideal LA: Cu2+ ratio as 4:1, the optimal heating temperature as 170 °C, and the optimal heating time as one hour. For Tb@N-CDs, optimal synthesis conditions were established at a 1:3 ratio of o-phenylenediamine to Tb3+, a heating temperature of 220 °C, and a heating time of three hours (Fig.S10).

Reaction conditions for the interaction between the fluorescent system and CFX were also optimized, focusing on the LA@CuNCs-to-Tb@N-CDs ratio, incubation time, and pH value (Fig.S11). As shown in Fig.S11A, the fluorescence response (F670/F460) was maximized with an LA@CuNCs-to-Tb@N-CDs molar ratio of 5:1. Fig.S11B demonstrates that the optimal reaction time was three minutes, after which fluorescence readings stabilized. Additionally, pH optimization for the fluorometric detection of CFX is shown in Fig.S11C, with the optimal pH for the interaction between CFX and the LA@CuNCs/Tb@N-CDs system determined to be 6.8. At lower pH values, fluorescence response decreases due to protonation of amino groups on the N-CDs surface, which interferes with coordination to Tb3+ ions, thereby reducing sensitivity and efficiency. In alkaline conditions, Tb3+ may precipitate, leading to suboptimal reaction conditions.

Several studies have documented the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effect in thiolated nanoclusters triggered by Tb3+ ions. This phenomenon is primarily attributed to the coordination interaction between carboxyl groups on the surface of LA and Tb3+ ions, facilitating aggregation and enhancing the nanoclusters' emission properties (Mu, Zhang, Huang, Jia, & Q., 2023; Liu, Wang, et al., 2024). Additionally, metal doping of N-CDs with Tb3+ has been shown to improve their electronic and photoluminescence properties, an effect accelerated by interactions between electron-donor groups, such as NH₂, on N-CD surfaces and the electron-acceptor properties of Tb3+ ions (Alqahtani et al., 2023; Song et al., 2022).

To elucidate the interaction mechanism between LA@CuNCs and Tb@N-CDs, morphological and spectroscopic analyses were conducted (Fig.S12). Fig.S12A shows TEM images of LA@CuNCs (negatively charged) after mixing with Tb@N-CDs (positively charged), where the average particle size increased from 2.1 ± 0.08 nm to 76.3 ± 0.87 nm, indicating aggregation of the LA@CuNCs probe. In Fig. S12B, the UV/Vis spectra of LA@CuNCs alone and mixed with increasing amounts of Tb@N-CDs reveal rising absorbance values with more Tb@N-CDs. This increase is likely due to enhanced light scattering from aggregated nanoclusters, as larger particle sizes amplify scattering effects. Additionally, coordination between Tb3+ ions and surface functional groups on N-CDs may modify the nanoclusters' electronic environment, further enhancing absorbance.

Fig.S12C shows the fluorescence spectra of LA@CuNCs, Tb-LA@CuNCs, LA@CuNCs/N-CD, Tb@N-CDs, and LA@CuNCs/Tb@N-CDs. The LA@CuNCs probe, which exhibits weak fluorescence, shows significant fluorescence enhancement upon the introduction of Tb3+ (forming Tb-LA@CuNCs). Notably, the fluorescence of N-CDs at 460 nm remained stable with LA@CuNCs addition but was enhanced with the addition of Tb3+ ions. Fig.S12D presents fluorescence emission spectra of LA@CuNCs/Tb@N-CDs at various excitation wavelengths, showing optimal fluorescence emission at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm.

Fig.S13A presents the UV/Vis absorption spectra of LA@CuNCs/Tb@N-CDs alongside the fluorescence emission spectrum of Tb@N-CDs. The spectral overlap suggests the presence of Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE) between LA@CuNCs and Tb@N-CDs. To distinguish between FRET and IFE, fluorescence lifetimes of LA@CuNCs were recorded before and after blending with Tb@N-CDs (Fig.S13B). The fluorescence lifetime of Tb@N-CDs increased from 6.87 ns to 8.15 ns upon mixing with LA@CuNCs, indicating FRET between them (Deng et al., 2023).

To further confirm electrostatic interactions between LA@CuNCs and Tb@N-CDs, Zeta potential measurements were performed (Fig.S13C). The Zeta potential of N-CDs was found to be −26.7 mV, reflecting a negative charge due to amino groups on the surface. Upon doping with Tb3+ ions, the Zeta potential shifted to +5.7 mV. LA@CuNCs exhibited a surface charge of −13.6 mV, attributed to LA. When LA@CuNCs and Tb@N-CDs were mixed, the surface charge shifted to −3.4 mV, suggesting enhanced interaction between the LA on the CuNCs surface and Tb3+ ions on the N-CDs surface.

The LA@CuNCs/Tb@N-CDs fluorescent probe was employed to detect fluoroquinolones (FQs) using ciprofloxacin (CFX) as a model compound under optimized conditions. Fig. 4A illustrates the effect of successive additions of CFX on the fluorescence emission of LA@CuNCs/Tb@N-CDs within the linear range of 0.05–120 μmol L−1. The fluorescence intensity at 460 nm increased, while the intensity at 670 nm decreased. These changes are attributed to the disruption of AIEE and FRET mechanisms due to the interaction between CFX and Tb3+ ions. This coordination interaction also weakens the attachment of Tb3+ to the N-CDs surface, resulting in a hypsochromic shift.

Fig. 4B shows the calibration plot, displaying the relationship between the fluorescence ratio (F670/F460) and CFX concentration. As the CFX concentration increased, the fluorescence ratio also rose. The linear regression equation describing this relationship is (F670/F460) = 0.62 + 0.012 [CFX], with an R2 value of 0.9984. The limit of detection (LOD), calculated with a signal-to-noise (S/N) ratio of 3:1, was determined to be 0.0016 μmol L−1. The standard deviation of the blank was 0.00001, and the LOD was calculated using the formula: LOD = 3 × (standard deviation of blank readings / slope of calibration plot). Furthermore, the limit of quantitation (LOQ), calculated with an S/N ratio of 10:1, was 0.008 μmol L−1. The analytical performance of the fluorescent probe was compared to other fluorometric sensors for CFX detection, as summarized in Table S1. The as-fabricated probe offers a wide linear dynamic range (LDR), low LOD, rapid detection capability, and high reliability, demonstrating its potential as an effective tool for CFX detection.

The stability of the fluorescent system was evaluated by storing the hybrid probe at −4 °C for approximately 25 days. The probe retained about 94.67 % of its initial fluorescence response, indicating strong stability. Reproducibility (precision) of the system was further assessed by preparing six batches under identical conditions. The results, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 3.56 %, demonstrate the high precision of the fabricated hybrid.

The selectivity of the LA@CuNCs/Tb@N-CDs fluorescent probe was evaluated by introducing various potential interferents into the probe solution, including 200 μmol L−1 of antibiotics (tetracycline hydrochloride, ampicillin, sulfamethoxazole, oxytetracycline, sulfamethazine), 250 μmol L−1 of common pesticides (cyhalothrin, flonicamid, dinotefuran, chlormequat, propargite, bifenthrin, cypermethrin, novaluron, boscalid), and 400 μmol L−1 of metal ions (K+, Na+, Mg2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Ag+, Fe3+). As shown in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence ratios displayed a significant increase in the presence of 30 μmol L−1 CFX, confirming that the fluorescent probe exhibits strong selectivity for CFX detection.

The LA@CuNCs/Tb@N-CDs fluorescent system was validated for detecting CFX in complex samples such as milk and urine using the standard addition method. Various amounts of CFX were spiked into the original samples, with percentage recoveries ranging from 97.7 % to 103.8 % and a relative standard deviation (RSD) not exceeding 3.79 %. These results were cross-referenced with the HPLC/UV method to confirm the accuracy of the fluorometric approach, demonstrating its effectiveness for detecting CFX in milk and urine samples (Table 1 ). The newly developed fluorometric method was also evaluated against existing regulatory limits set by international food safety authorities. According to the European Union (Regulation EC No 37/2010), Codex Alimentarius, and FDA, the maximum residue limit (MRL) for CFX in milk is 0.1 mg L−1. The developed method, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.0016 μmol L−1 (0.0053 mg L−1), is significantly more sensitive than the regulatory threshold. This high sensitivity ensures the method's capability to detect CFX at concentrations well below the MRL, facilitating early detection and regulatory compliance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quality control of fluorometric and HPLC/UV methods

ce:section.ce:para: Calibration curves were generated using standard solutions of CFX, with concentrations ranging from 0.008 to 120 μmol L−1 for the fluorometric method and 0.014 to 130 μmol L−1 for the HPLC method. The determination coefficients (R2) were found to be 0.9984 and 0.9965 for the fluorometric and HPLC methods, respectively, indicating excellent linearity for both approaches.

The precision of the fluorometric and HPLC methods was evaluated by analyzing four replicate samples of spiked milk and urine at three concentration levels (low, medium, and high). The relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated for both intra-day and inter-day measurements, with RSD values consistently below 3.79 % for the fluorometric method and below 4.79 % for the HPLC method. These results indicate robust precision for both analytical approaches.

To evaluate the accuracy of the method, known amounts of CFX were spiked into blank milk and urine samples at three concentration levels. The samples were processed using both the fluorometric and HPLC methods, and percentage recoveries were calculated. Recoveries ranged from 97.7 % to 103.8 % for the fluorometric method and from 95.0 % to 105.4 % for the HPLC method. These results confirm the accuracy of the fluorometric method.

The limits of detection (LOD) and quantitation (LOQ) were determined based on signal-to-noise ratios of 3:1 and 10:1, respectively. For the fluorometric method, the LOD and LOQ for CFX were calculated to be 1.6 nmol L−1 and 0.008 μmol L−1, respectively. For the HPLC/UV method, the LOD and LOQ for CFX were 2.18 nmol L−1 and 0.014 μmol L−1, respectively.

In this study, a novel approach was developed for the reliable and sensitive fluorometric detection of quinolones, using ciprofloxacin (CFX) as a model compound. The probe is based on electrostatic interactions between negatively charged LA@CuNCs and positively charged Tb@N-CDs. The addition of Tb@N-CDs induces aggregation of LA@CuNCs through aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE), resulting in strong fluorescence emission from LA@CuNCs. Concurrently, the fluorescence emission of Tb@N-CDs decreases due to Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between LA@CuNCs and Tb@N-CDs. In the presence of CFX, a potent chelator of Tb3+ ions, the fluorescence emission of LA@CuNCs at 670 nm decreases, while the emission of Tb@N-CDs at 460 nm increases, due to the disruption of both FRET and AIEE mechanisms. This approach offers a distinct advantage over traditional nanostructure design methods.
